name: Chilean Central Valley
fireFrequency: High — exacerbated by eucalyptus and pine plantations
dominantVegetation: Mediterranean scrub, eucalyptus plantations, pine plantations
seasonalPattern: Austral summer (December–March) brings hot, dry conditions to central Chile. A megadrought since 2010 — the worst in 1,000 years — has dramatically increased fire risk. Exotic timber plantations (eucalyptus and pine) burn with extreme intensity and have replaced much of the native matorral shrubland, creating a more fire-prone landscape.
description: Central Chile faces a growing wildfire crisis driven by the convergence of a decade-long megadrought, vast industrial timber plantations, and a rapidly warming Mediterranean climate. The 2017 fire season was Chile's worst in modern history, burning 614,000 hectares and killing 11 people. Eucalyptus and pine plantations — which cover over 3 million hectares — burn with extreme intensity and are rapidly replacing native vegetation. Chile has lost more native forest to fire in the last decade than in the previous century.
